events.text: The patient is a 58-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is of Polish-American descent, a retired steel mill worker from Pittsburgh, PA. He has a 25 pack-year smoking history (quit 12 years ago), moderate alcohol use (2-3 drinks/week), BMI 27.4, ECOG performance status 1 at diagnosis. Past medical history includes hypertension (controlled on lisinopril 10mg daily), hyperlipidemia (on atorvastatin 40mg daily), and type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled, HbA1c 6.8%). No family history of colorectal cancer. He is married with two adult children.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3 N2a M1a) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE SIGMOID COLON (ICD-O-3 site code C18.7, histology code 8140/3: Adenocarcinoma, NOS). Summary stage: Distant (metastatic). Primary tumor location: sigmoid colon, 18 cm from anal verge by colonoscopy. Synchronous liver metastases: 4 lesions in bilateral hepatic lobes (largest 3.2 cm in right lobe segment VI), consistent with M1a disease (liver-only at initial staging). Relevant biomarkers: KRAS exon 2 codon 12 G12D mutation (pathogenic); NRAS wild-type; BRAF wild-type (non-V600E); HER2 not amplified; MMR proficient / microsatellite stable (pMMR/MSS) by IHC (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+); CEA at diagnosis: 148.2 ng/mL; CA 19-9: 312 U/mL. Confirmation: Positive histology (colonoscopic biopsy of sigmoid mass). Tumor size (primary): 4.1 cm. Primary tumor was not resected at time of diagnosis (synchronous presentation with unresectable liver mets). Tumor sidedness: Left-sided. Tumor differentiation: Moderately differentiated.

At age 58 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from colonoscopic biopsy of sigmoid colon mass. Gross description: Multiple biopsy fragments, 0.3-0.8 cm in aggregate. Microscopic findings: Invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with tubular and focal cribriform architecture. Tumor cells with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, and moderate cytoplasm. Intraluminal necrotic debris present (dirty necrosis). No signet ring cell features. Lymphovascular invasion: Cannot be assessed on biopsy. Perineural invasion: Cannot be assessed on biopsy. IHC panel: CDX2 positive (diffuse, strong), CK20 positive, CK7 negative, consistent with colorectal primary. MMR proteins: MLH1 retained, MSH2 retained, MSH6 retained, PMS2 retained (pMMR/MSS). Diagnosis: Invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV and oral contrast (staging CT CAP) performed. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Circumferential sigmoid colon wall thickening measuring 4.1 x 3.8 cm with mucosal enhancement and pericolonic fat stranding. Several pericolonic lymph nodes enlarged up to 1.5 cm (N2a disease). No evidence of local invasion beyond colonic wall. LIVER: Four hepatic lesions identified: (1) Segment VI right lobe, 3.2 x 2.8 cm, hypoenhancing with peripheral rim enhancement; (2) Segment IVb, 1.8 x 1.6 cm; (3) Segment II left lobe, 1.4 x 1.1 cm; (4) Segment VII, 0.9 x 0.8 cm. Imaging characteristics consistent with metastatic deposits. LUNGS: No pulmonary nodules identified. LYMPH NODES: No distant lymphadenopathy. PERITONEUM: No peritoneal carcinomatosis. BONES: No osseous metastases. Impression: Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. Potentially resectable liver disease contingent on chemotherapy response. No extrahepatic metastases identified. Baseline imaging for treatment response assessment.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for colorectal adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the sigmoid colon (primary tumor biopsy). Platform: Foundation One CDx (comprehensive genomic profiling). Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 5 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12D mutation (allele frequency 42%, pathogenic, known oncogenic driver; RAS pathway activator; excludes benefit from EGFR-targeted therapies); TP53 p.R248W mutation (allele frequency 48%, pathogenic, loss-of-function); APC p.E1554* nonsense mutation (allele frequency 51%, pathogenic, truncating, loss of tumor suppressor function). Co-mutations and additional findings: SMAD4 p.R361H mutation (allele frequency 38%, likely pathogenic, TGF-beta pathway alteration; may be associated with worse prognosis in stage IV CRC); PIK3CA exon 20 H1047R mutation not detected; BRAF V600E not detected; NRAS exon 3 codon 61 wild-type; ERBB2 (HER2) copy number: not amplified (CN 2.1); MET copy number: not amplified; CDKN2A homozygous deletion detected; FBXW7 p.R505C mutation (allele frequency 22%, likely pathogenic); Copy number alterations: chromosome 8q gain (MYC locus), chromosome 18q loss (SMAD2/SMAD4 locus), chromosome 20q gain. Gene fusions: None detected. Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 14% (intermediate). POLE/POLD1: wild-type. RET: wild-type. NTRK1/2/3 fusions: not detected. Summary: KRAS G12D mutant, BRAF wild-type, pMMR/MSS colorectal adenocarcinoma. Not a candidate for anti-EGFR therapy. No targetable alterations identified beyond standard chemotherapy regimens.

At age 58 years, the patient had an initial oncology consultation and multidisciplinary tumor board review. Diagnosis: Stage IV (M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases. Tumor board discussion: Liver disease is technically resectable but borderline given bilobar involvement. Decision to pursue conversion chemotherapy with FOLFOX plus bevacizumab with intent to reassess hepatic resectability after 4-6 cycles. Primary tumor is asymptomatic; no immediate need for diverting surgery. CEA 148.2, CA 19-9 312. Patient counseled on goals of therapy (potentially curative intent if conversion to resectable disease achieved), treatment side effects (peripheral neuropathy with oxaliplatin, hypertension with bevacizumab, myelosuppression), and the importance of monitoring hepatic function. Patient is agreeable and motivated. ECOG PS 1. Plan: Initiate FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab. Port-a-cath placement arranged. Baseline labs reviewed: CBC WNL, CMP WNL, LFTs mildly elevated (ALT 52, AST 48, likely from liver metastases). Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Baseline blood pressure 128/78 mmHg on lisinopril.

At age 58 years, the patient underwent port-a-cath placement (subcutaneous implantable venous access device, right chest wall). Procedure performed by interventional radiology. No intraoperative complications. Port accessed and flushed without difficulty. Chest X-ray post-procedure confirmed appropriate port position, no pneumothorax.

At age 58 years, the patient initiated palliative/conversion-intent systemic chemotherapy with modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab (Cycle 1 Day 1). Regimen: Bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV over 30-90 min; Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours (concurrent); 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus; 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length: 14 days. Premedications: Ondansetron 8 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, palonosetron for delayed nausea prophylaxis. Cycle 1 administered without acute infusion reactions. Patient discharged home with 5-FU pump and antiemetic prescriptions.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into treatment, after Cycle 3 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient developed Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin-induced). Symptoms: Mild tingling and numbness in fingertips, bilateral, worse with cold exposure (cold-induced dysesthesias characteristic of acute oxaliplatin neurotoxicity). Also noted: Grade 1 fatigue, Grade 1 nausea (well-controlled with ondansetron). No dose reduction required at this time. Counseled on cold avoidance and use of gloves. Blood pressure noted at this visit: 148/92 mmHg -- new Grade 1 hypertension likely related to bevacizumab. Lisinopril dose increased from 10 mg to 20 mg daily. Blood pressure target: <140/90 mmHg.

At age 58 years (after 4 cycles of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 2 months after treatment initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast for interim response assessment. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass mildly decreased in size, now measuring 3.6 x 3.2 cm (previously 4.1 x 3.8 cm). Pericolonic lymph nodes decreased in number and size (largest now 0.9 cm). LIVER: All four hepatic lesions demonstrate interval decrease in size: (1) Segment VI lesion now 2.6 x 2.2 cm (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm); (2) Segment IVb now 1.3 x 1.2 cm (previously 1.8 x 1.6 cm); (3) Segment II now 1.0 x 0.9 cm (previously 1.4 x 1.1 cm); (4) Segment VII now 0.7 x 0.6 cm (previously 0.9 x 0.8 cm). LUNGS: No new pulmonary nodules. No new metastatic disease. Response assessment by RECIST 1.1: Partial response (PR). Sum of target lesion diameters decreased by 28% from baseline. CEA: 67.4 ng/mL (down from 148.2 at baseline). Impression: Partial response to FOLFOX/bevacizumab. Hepatic disease remains bilobar. Multidisciplinary reassessment recommended after completion of planned 6 additional cycles.

At age 58 years (after 4 cycles, interim imaging review), the patient had an oncology office visit for treatment assessment. Cancer present, responding to therapy. Imaging confirms partial response with 28% reduction in hepatic and primary tumor burden. CEA trending down. Patient tolerating treatment reasonably well with Grade 1 neuropathy (fingertip tingling, cold dysesthesias), Grade 1 hypertension now better controlled on increased lisinopril dose (BP today 136/84), Grade 1 fatigue. Performance status remains ECOG 1. Plan: Continue FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for planned total of 12 cycles. Repeat tumor board review after Cycle 8 to reassess hepatic resectability. Neuropathy monitoring ongoing -- will reduce oxaliplatin dose by 25% or discontinue if neuropathy progresses to Grade 2 or higher. Patient and spouse in good spirits, motivated to continue.

At age 58 years (approximately 5 months into treatment, after Cycle 7 of FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (cumulative oxaliplatin-induced neurotoxicity). Symptoms: Persistent numbness and tingling in fingers and toes bilaterally, now present at rest (no longer only cold-triggered), mild difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning shirts). Neurologic examination: Decreased vibratory sense in bilateral great toes. Action taken: Oxaliplatin dose reduced by 25% (from 85 mg/m2 to 64 mg/m2) per ASCO guidelines. Patient counseled on cumulative neuropathy risk. Bevacizumab and 5-FU doses maintained. Blood pressure: 134/82 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg -- well-controlled. Grade 1 alopecia noted (partial). Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, mild, does not require dose reduction. Neurology referral placed for neuropathy evaluation and management.

At age 58 years (after 8 cycles of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 4 months after treatment initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass now 3.1 x 2.7 cm (previously 3.6 x 3.2 cm post-Cycle 4), mild further reduction. Pericolonic nodes no longer enlarged. LIVER: Further reduction in all four lesions: (1) Segment VI: 2.0 x 1.7 cm; (2) Segment IVb: 0.9 x 0.8 cm; (3) Segment II: 0.7 x 0.6 cm; (4) Segment VII: 0.5 x 0.4 cm. LUNGS: No pulmonary nodules identified. No new distant metastases. Response assessment (RECIST 1.1): Continued partial response, cumulative 42% decrease in sum of target lesion diameters from baseline. CEA: 38.1 ng/mL (continued downtrend). Impression: Ongoing partial response to FOLFOX/bevacizumab. Liver disease showing continued treatment response. Tumor board review recommended: hepatic metastasectomy may be feasible for the dominant Segment VI lesion; however, Segment IVb, II, and VII lesions remain present and multifocal resection carries high risk. Consider continued chemotherapy vs. ablative approaches.

At age 58 years (multidisciplinary tumor board re-review after 8 cycles), the patient was discussed at the hepatobiliary and colorectal oncology multidisciplinary tumor board. Cancer present, responding to therapy. Discussion: Continued hepatic response but four separate liver lesions remain across bilateral lobes. Hepatic Surgery assessment: Resection technically feasible with R0 potential for Segment VI lesion; however, Segments II and IVb involvement would require extensive hepatectomy with marginal future liver remnant (FLR estimated 28%). FOLFOX-associated hepatotoxicity (sinusoidal obstruction) also a concern given 8-cycle exposure. Decision: Continue chemotherapy for 4 additional cycles (total 12 planned) before further resectability reassessment. Consider portal vein embolization if FLR remains marginal. If maximal response achieved, reassess for staged hepatectomy vs. radiofrequency ablation for smaller lesions. Neuropathy: Grade 2, oxaliplatin dose-reduced 25%. Will proceed with final 4 cycles at reduced dose. Patient agrees with plan.

At age 58 years (after completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, approximately 6 months after treatment initiation), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for end-of-induction response assessment. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass now 2.8 x 2.4 cm with decreased mural thickening and improved mucosal appearance. Pericolonic nodes resolved. LIVER: (1) Segment VI: 1.6 x 1.4 cm (significantly reduced); (2) Segment IVb: 0.7 x 0.5 cm (near-radiographic CR); (3) Segment II: 0.5 x 0.4 cm (near-radiographic CR); (4) Segment VII: Not definitively visualized (possible radiographic CR). Overall RECIST response: Confirmed partial response, 52% reduction in target lesion sum from baseline. CEA: 22.8 ng/mL (continued downtrend from 148.2 baseline). CA 19-9: 89 U/mL (down from 312 baseline). LUNGS: No pulmonary nodules. No new distant metastatic disease. Impression: Best response to induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab: confirmed partial response. Sigmoid primary tumor reduced but residual disease present. Hepatic disease significantly reduced with three of four lesions near or at radiographic complete response. Segment VI dominant lesion remains identifiable.

At age 58 years (completion of 12 cycles of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, end-of-induction assessment), the patient had an oncology visit for treatment transition planning. Cancer present, with partial response to induction therapy. Per plan, oxaliplatin discontinued after 12 cycles due to cumulative neurotoxicity and reaching planned treatment endpoint. Neuropathy currently Grade 2, persistent at rest, bilateral hands and feet -- improvement expected over time following oxaliplatin discontinuation. Blood pressure: 138/86 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg, acceptable control. Performance status: ECOG 1. Tumor board reviewed imaging: residual sigmoid primary tumor (likely still harboring viable tumor given partial response only), Segment VI liver lesion 1.6 cm, three smaller lesions near-CR. Decision: Transition to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (per OPTIMOX strategy -- capecitabine considered but patient preference for infusional 5-FU given adequate port access). Will continue bevacizumab to maintain disease control. Re-evaluate for surgical consolidation at 3 months of maintenance if neuropathy improves. Patient counseled on maintenance strategy and prognosis.

At age 58 years (6 months after initial diagnosis), the patient transitioned to maintenance systemic therapy with 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (simplified LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab). Regimen: Bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours; 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus followed by 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length: 14 days (biweekly). Oxaliplatin omitted due to cumulative Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. Rationale: Maintenance strategy per OPTIMOX1 trial concept -- oxaliplatin holiday, continuation of 5-FU/bevacizumab backbone to consolidate response and delay progression. Goal: Maintain partial response achieved with induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab, preserve quality of life, allow neuropathy recovery.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into maintenance therapy, 8 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a follow-up neurology appointment for oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy. Assessment: Neuropathy has improved from Grade 2 to Grade 1 following oxaliplatin discontinuation. Patient reports residual mild tingling in fingertips and toes but improved fine motor function (can button shirt again without difficulty). Neurologic exam: Reduced but improved vibratory sense in toes. Nerve conduction studies: Mild sensory neuropathy pattern consistent with oxaliplatin-induced injury. Recommendation: Continue monitoring, physical therapy for balance exercises, no additional pharmacologic intervention needed at this time. Prognosis for further gradual improvement over 6-12 months. Blood pressure on maintenance: 132/80 mmHg, well-controlled. Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome mild/stable. Patient tolerating maintenance well with Grade 1 fatigue.

At age 58 years (approximately 10 months from initial diagnosis, after approximately 4 months of maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for routine response surveillance. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass appears stable compared to prior CT, measuring 2.9 x 2.5 cm (minimally changed from 2.8 x 2.4 cm). No bowel obstruction. LIVER: Segment VI lesion: 2.1 x 1.8 cm -- INCREASED from 1.6 x 1.4 cm (31% increase, unequivocal progression). Segment IVb: 1.0 x 0.9 cm -- new apparent growth from 0.7 x 0.5 cm. Segment II: 0.6 x 0.5 cm -- minimally changed. LUNGS: NEW FINDINGS: Three new bilateral pulmonary nodules identified: (1) Right lower lobe, 0.8 x 0.7 cm; (2) Left upper lobe, 0.6 x 0.5 cm; (3) Right middle lobe, 0.5 x 0.4 cm. Morphology and clinical context consistent with pulmonary metastases. IMPRESSION: Disease progression by RECIST 1.1 -- new pulmonary metastases (new lesion = PD regardless of primary/hepatic lesion changes) and hepatic progression. This represents a significant change from prior partial response. CEA: 67.3 ng/mL (up from 22.8 at end of induction). CA 19-9: 187 U/mL (up from 89). Clinical urgency: Prompt oncology reassessment and second-line therapy planning indicated.

At age 58 years (10 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an urgent oncology visit following surveillance imaging demonstrating disease progression. Cancer present, progressing. CEA risen to 67.3 (previously 22.8). New pulmonary metastases identified (three bilateral nodules) plus hepatic progression of Segment VI and IVb lesions. This represents progression on maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab, consistent with oxaliplatin-free interval reintroduction pattern. Assessment: Second-line disease progression; patient is now stage IV M1b (liver and lung metastases). Performance status: ECOG 1 (maintained). Labs reviewed: CBC -- mild anemia (Hgb 10.8), ANC 2.1, platelets 198; CMP -- LFTs mildly elevated (ALT 68, AST 62, likely from hepatic progression). Creatinine 0.9. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.18 (borderline, monitoring for proteinuria given continued bevacizumab exposure). Discussion: Second-line therapy options reviewed including FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab (ECOG E3200 precedent) vs. FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (RAISE trial, demonstrated OS benefit with ramucirumab vs. placebo in mCRC post-FOLFOX). Given biologic durability question with continued bevacizumab, decision made to switch to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Patient counseled regarding prognosis and realistic goals of care in second-line setting. Patient understands goals are palliative, wishes to continue aggressive treatment.

At age 58 years (10 months from initial diagnosis), the patient initiated second-line systemic chemotherapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cycle 1 Day 1). Regimen: Ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV over 60 min (given first); Irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 minutes; Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours (concurrent with irinotecan); 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus; 5-Fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length: 14 days. Premedications: Ondansetron 8 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, atropine 0.25 mg IV (for irinotecan-related cholinergic syndrome prophylaxis). Loperamide prescription provided for delayed diarrhea management. Rationale: RAISE trial demonstrated OS benefit of ramucirumab plus FOLFIRI vs. FOLFIRI alone in second-line mCRC (median OS 13.3 vs. 11.7 months, HR 0.844, p=0.022). Prior anti-VEGF therapy with bevacizumab; class switch to anti-VEGFR2 antibody (ramucirumab). Baseline blood pressure: 142/90 mmHg (mild elevation likely residual bevacizumab effect plus inherent hypertension risk).

At age 58 years (approximately 1 month into second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab therapy, 11 months from diagnosis), the patient developed Grade 2 hypertension exacerbation attributed to ramucirumab (VEGFR2 inhibition). Blood pressure readings in clinic: 158/96 mmHg, 162/98 mmHg. Home monitoring confirmed persistent elevation. This represents new-onset Grade 2 hypertension despite lisinopril 20 mg daily. Also noted: Grade 2 diarrhea (irinotecan-related), requiring loperamide 4 mg initially then 2 mg after each loose stool, up to 16 mg/day -- adequately controlled on this regimen. Grade 1 alopecia (expected with irinotecan). Grade 1 fatigue. UGT1A1 genotype: heterozygous *28/*1 (intermediate metabolizer), consistent with moderate irinotecan sensitivity -- no dose reduction required at current dose. Management of hypertension: Amlodipine 5 mg daily added to lisinopril 20 mg. Blood pressure target <140/90 mmHg. Recheck in 2 weeks. Ramucirumab dose held for one cycle pending blood pressure control.

At age 58 years (approximately 2 months into second-line therapy, 12 months from diagnosis), the patient returned for blood pressure reassessment and Cycle 3 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Blood pressure: 136/84 mmHg on dual antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg). Hypertension now Grade 1 -- adequately controlled. Ramucirumab dose resuming at full dose per protocol (held one cycle). Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.22 (acceptable, <2.0 threshold for ramucirumab hold). Diarrhea: Grade 1, managed with occasional loperamide. Neuropathy: Continued Grade 1 residual oxaliplatin neuropathy (mild tingling fingertips), not worsening with FOLFIRI (irinotecan does not cause neuropathy). Fatigue: Grade 1, manageable. Performance status: ECOG 1. CBC: Hgb 10.2 (mild anemia, Grade 1), ANC 1.8 (Grade 1 neutropenia, no febrile neutropenia), platelets 178. No dose reductions required at this time.

At age 58 years (approximately 4 months into second-line therapy, 14 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for response assessment on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass: 2.7 x 2.3 cm (minimally changed, stable). No bowel obstruction, no pericolonic complications. LIVER: Segment VI lesion: 1.8 x 1.5 cm (decreased from 2.1 x 1.8 cm, improving); Segment IVb: 0.8 x 0.7 cm (decreased from 1.0 x 0.9 cm, improving); Segment II: 0.5 x 0.4 cm (stable/near-CR). LUNGS: Right lower lobe nodule: 0.7 x 0.6 cm (minimally decreased from 0.8 x 0.7 cm); Left upper lobe nodule: 0.5 x 0.5 cm (stable); Right middle lobe nodule: 0.4 x 0.3 cm (stable to slightly decreased). No new pulmonary nodules. No new metastatic sites identified. Response assessment by RECIST 1.1: Partial response -- 18% decrease in sum of target lesion diameters from nadir at progression. Overall assessment: Favorable response to FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with hepatic and pulmonary disease stabilization and partial response. CEA: 44.1 ng/mL (decreased from 67.3 at progression). CA 19-9: 134 U/mL (decreased from 187).

At age 58 years (14 months from initial diagnosis, 4 months into second-line therapy), the patient had an oncology visit for treatment response review. Cancer present, responding to second-line therapy. Imaging demonstrates partial response to FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with hepatic and pulmonary disease responding. CEA declining (44 from nadir 67). Patient tolerating treatment with manageable toxicity profile: Grade 1 diarrhea (loperamide as needed), Grade 1 hypertension (controlled on dual antihypertensive therapy), Grade 1 fatigue, residual Grade 1 oxaliplatin neuropathy (stable, not worsening). Performance status: ECOG 1. Quality of life: Patient reports continued ability to perform light household activities, occasional golf (modified), maintaining social functioning. He is retired and spends time with grandchildren. Psychosocial: Patient and wife remain engaged. Discussed clinical trial options -- no immediately available trials matching his profile at this institution. Plan: Continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Reassess imaging in 8 weeks. Ongoing antihypertensive management. Palliative care referral offered; patient defers at this time but open to future referral.

At age 59 years (approximately 7 months into second-line therapy, 17 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast for continued response surveillance. Findings: PRIMARY TUMOR: Sigmoid colon mass: 2.8 x 2.5 cm (minimally increased, borderline stable vs. progression). Mild pericolonic haziness noted. LIVER: Segment VI lesion: 1.9 x 1.7 cm (minimally increased from 1.8 x 1.5 cm, borderline stable); Segment IVb: 0.9 x 0.8 cm (minimally increased); Segment II: 0.6 x 0.5 cm (minimally increased). LUNGS: Right lower lobe nodule: 0.8 x 0.7 cm (stable); Left upper lobe nodule: 0.6 x 0.5 cm (minimal increase); Right middle lobe nodule: 0.4 x 0.4 cm (stable). No new pulmonary nodules or new sites of metastatic disease. Response assessment (RECIST 1.1): Stable disease (SD) -- no unequivocal progression, no new lesions, no significant decrease. Sum of target lesions borderline increased by 8% from prior nadir (not meeting 20% threshold for PD). CEA: 51.8 ng/mL (slight increase from 44.1). CA 19-9: 162 U/mL. Impression: Best response was PR, currently stable disease. Continued treatment monitoring recommended; close interval imaging to confirm stability vs. early progression.

At age 59 years (17 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncology visit for ongoing disease management. Cancer present, stable disease on second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Imaging shows stable disease with borderline trend towards minimal progression. CEA trending slightly upward (51.8 vs. 44.1). Performance status: ECOG 1. Cumulative irinotecan toxicity: Grade 1 diarrhea (managed), Grade 1 fatigue. Hypertension: BP 138/86 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg, acceptable. Neuropathy: Grade 1 residual (oxaliplatin), stable, no worsening. Hematologic: Hgb 10.0, ANC 1.6, plt 165 -- mild myelosuppression, no febrile neutropenia events. Discussed disease trajectory with patient. Patient continues to have good quality of life. Reviewed third-line options in anticipation of future progression: regorafenib (CORRECT trial) or TAS-102/trifluridine-tipiracil (RECOURSE trial) are standard options in chemorefractory mCRC. No NTRK fusion, no HER2 amplification, no BRAF V600E -- no targeted therapy options available. Plan: Continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with close monitoring. Repeat imaging in 6 weeks given borderline findings.

At age 59 years (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a pathology result from CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule (0.8 cm), performed to confirm metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma and obtain tissue for repeat molecular profiling (ctDNA and tissue re-biopsy for potential acquired resistance mechanisms). Gross: Three core needle biopsy fragments, 0.8-1.2 cm. Microscopic: Sections show moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with morphology consistent with metastatic colorectal primary. Tumor cells arranged in glandular structures with focal dirty necrosis. IHC: CK20 positive (strong diffuse), CDX2 positive (moderate diffuse), CK7 negative, TTF-1 negative. Findings consistent with metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma to lung (not a primary lung adenocarcinoma). Note: Specimen submitted for repeat next-generation sequencing to evaluate for acquired resistance mutations. Diagnosis: Metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary. Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule biopsy.

At age 59 years (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had next-generation sequencing performed on a repeat biopsy specimen obtained from the right lower lobe pulmonary metastasis (post-treatment specimen, obtained after FOLFOX and FOLFIRI exposure). Platform: Foundation One CDx. TMB: 6 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (unchanged from baseline). RETAINED alterations from baseline: KRAS p.G12D (allele frequency 47%, retained oncogenic driver); TP53 p.R248W (allele frequency 51%, retained); APC p.E1554* (allele frequency 48%, retained); SMAD4 p.R361H (allele frequency 41%, retained); CDKN2A homozygous deletion (retained). NEW/ACQUIRED alterations compared to baseline primary tumor biopsy: KRAS p.G12V secondary subclonal mutation (allele frequency 11%, likely acquired, potential mechanism of resistance or tumor heterogeneity); PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (allele frequency 18%, likely acquired or newly detectable, PI3K pathway activation); MYC amplification (copy number gain, CN 8.2, increased from CN ~3 at baseline); RB1 p.Q494* truncating mutation (allele frequency 14%, likely acquired, loss of tumor suppressor). ERBB2: not amplified. VEGFR2/KDR: no amplification or activating mutation (ramucirumab target unaltered). FUSIONS: None detected. Summary: Evolution of molecular landscape with acquisition of potential resistance mechanisms (PIK3CA E545K, RB1 loss, MYC amplification, subclonal KRAS G12V). MSS phenotype retained. No actionable targetable alterations emerge beyond standard therapy. Clinical relevance: Molecular evolution consistent with clonal selection under chemotherapy pressure.

At age 59 years (18-19 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a comprehensive oncology visit integrating repeat molecular profiling results with clinical status. Cancer present, with stable disease on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab though borderline trend toward progression. Repeat NGS from pulmonary metastasis shows molecular evolution: PIK3CA E545K acquisition, MYC amplification, RB1 loss -- consistent with clonal evolution under chemotherapy selection. KRAS G12D retained as dominant clone; new KRAS G12V subclone identified suggesting intratumoral heterogeneity. MSS status unchanged -- no immunotherapy opportunity. No HER2 amplification, no NTRK fusion, no actionable target identified. Performance status: ECOG 1. Patient remains functional, engaged, living at home. Updated goals of care discussion: Patient understands disease is incurable; primary goals are prolonging survival while maintaining quality of life and functional independence. Agreed to continue current regimen until clear radiographic progression given stable disease. Third-line options (regorafenib, TAS-102/tipiracil, or clinical trial) discussed for future planning. Patient expressed interest in clinical trial if eligible at time of next progression. Social work referral accepted at this visit. Palliative care referral accepted. Advanced care planning discussion initiated: patient has healthcare proxy (wife), completed advance directive.

scenario_blurb: Stage IV colorectal cancer with KRAS G12D mutation and synchronous liver metastases in a 58-year-old male. Initial treatment with FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for 12 cycles with partial response, then maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab. Progression at 10 months with new lung nodules, switched to FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. Develops oxaliplatin neuropathy and treatment-related hypertension.
